text: The surgeon uses an OptiView technique in the left upper quadrant, revealing extensive omental adhesions and a large piece of iPOM mesh covering the midline. A higher left upper quadrant port is established for camera access, and the surgeon employs a ligature device to dissect and remove the adhesions.

The surgeon uses an OptiView technique in the left upper quadrant, revealing extensive omental adhesions and a large piece of iPOM mesh covering the midline. A higher left upper quadrant port is placed, and the camera is inserted through it. The surgeon begins using a ligature device to dissect and remove the adhesions.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to carefully dissect adhesions along the abdominal wall, avoiding leaving any residual fat and minimizing bleeding risk due to the patient's cirrhosis.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to carefully dissect adhesions along the abdominal wall, avoiding leaving any residual fat and minimizing bleeding risk due to the patient's cirrhosis.

The surgeon clears additional adhesions to create more working space, revealing the falciform ligament that was not previously taken down during the ventral hernia repair. The dissection is directed towards and over the liver area.

The surgeon clears additional adhesions to create more working space, revealing the falciform ligament that was not previously taken down during the ventral hernia repair. The dissection is directed upwards and over the liver area.

The surgeon docks the robotic system, positioning the arms with a gentle hockey stick configuration. Arm number two is placed in the suprapubic region, preparing for further adhesiolysis in the surgical field.
The surgeon docks the robotic system, positioning arm number two in a suprapubic location, and prepares to continue adhesiolysis in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters adhesions near the hepatic flexure, which are attributed to a prior open liver resection. Visible tattoos on the tissue suggest previous endoscopic interventions.

The surgeon navigates through dense adhesions, which are attributed to a previous open liver resection. The camera captures the tattoo marking near the hepatic flexure, suggesting the site of an attempted endoscopic mucosal resection.

The surgeon navigates between the transverse colon and the falciform ligament, releasing the transverse colon from its adhesion to the anterior abdominal wall. The superior extent of the mesh is visible. The surgeon then attempts to create space between the colon and the stomach, with the posterior aspect of the stomach clearly visible.

The surgeon navigates between the transverse colon and the falciform ligament, releasing the transverse colon from its adhesion to the anterior abdominal wall. The superior extent of the mesh is visible. The surgeon then attempts to create space in the plane between the colon and the stomach, revealing the posterior aspect of the stomach.
The surgeon observes the fused lesser sac, likely due to previous surgeries, and prepares to start dissection at the iliocolic pedicle once the plane is better visualized.

The surgeon observes the fused lesser sac, likely due to previous surgeries, and begins working in this area. Once the plane is better visualized, the surgeon prepares to start dissection at the iliocolic pedicle.

The surgeon identifies the hepatic flexure plastered to the edge of the liver and begins dissecting the iliocolic pedicle, initiating a medial to lateral dissection. A large flat polyp is visible at the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the hepatic flexure plastered to the edge of the liver and begins the medial to lateral dissection of the iliocolic pedicle. The surgical field reveals a large flat polyp at the hepatic flexure, previously subjected to endoscopic biopsies and an attempted EMR, showing no dysplasia and tubal villus adenoma.
The surgeon works laterally underneath the iliocolic pedicle, thinning it out a few centimeters away from the superior mesenteric vessels to expose the artery and vein.

The surgeon works laterally underneath the iliocolic pedicle, thinning it out a few centimeters away from the superior mesenteric vessels. The artery and vein are exposed as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon identifies a thickened iliocolic pedicle and proceeds to separately ligate the artery and vein. The vein is isolated and prepared for ligation.
The surgeon isolates the thickened pedicle and proceeds to separately dissect the vein from the artery, focusing on the vein in this segment.

The surgeon carefully isolates and takes the ileocolic artery amidst fibrotic tissues, which are visibly thickened due to the patient's cirrhosis. Despite the challenging tissue environment, the vessel sealing appears adequate.

The surgeon encounters fibrotic tissue while dissecting around the ileocolic artery, a common finding in patients with cirrhosis. Despite the thickened tissue, the surgeon successfully isolates and takes the ileocolic artery without complications.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and observes the tattoo markings throughout the fibrotic retroperitoneum. The mesentery is marked to plan the transverse colon mesenteric transection, ensuring orientation is maintained during dissection.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and observes the tattoo markings throughout the fibrotic retroperitoneum. The mesentery is marked to plan the transverse colon mesenteric transection, ensuring proper orientation during the dissection.

The surgeon dissects up and over the right kidney, revealing fibrotic tissue and tattoo markings. The transverse colon resection line is marked out by manipulating the anterior leaflet, with the middle colic vessels visible in the area. The tissue is adhered to Girota's fascia, likely due to a previous endoscopic mucosal resection attempt.

The surgeon dissects over the right kidney, encountering fibrotic tissue and tattoo markings. The transverse colon resection line is marked on the anterior leaflet, with the middle colic vessels visible. The area is adhered to Girota's fascia, likely due to a previous endoscopic mucosal resection attempt.
The surgeon marks the planned distal margin on the colon and proceeds to enter the lesser sac, preparing to mobilize around the colonic flexure.
The surgeon marks the planned distal margin for resection, preparing to enter the lesser sac and maneuver around the flexure.

The surgeon places a sponge to manage minor bleeding and proceeds to take down the hepatic flexure, which is adhered to the bed of a previous liver resection and cholecystectomy. The thick tissues are dissected in layers.

The surgeon places a sponge to manage minor bleeding and proceeds to take down the hepatic flexure, which is adhered to the bed of a previous liver resection and cholecystectomy. The thick tissues are dissected in layers.

The surgeon follows the hepatic flexure, attempting to dissect deeper below the fibrotic layer on the liver's surface. The tissue is adhered, likely due to previous liver resection or attempted EMR, prompting the use of a ligature for dissection instead of scissors. The absence of a gallbladder and roux reconstruction is noted, with the liver visible on the opposite side of the colon.

The surgeon follows the hepatic flexure around, attempting to dissect deeper below the fibrotic layer on the liver's surface. Due to the adhesions from prior liver resection or attempted EMR, the surgeon uses a ligature for dissection instead of scissors. The visual field shows the liver adjacent to the colon, with no gallbladder or roux reconstruction present.

The surgeon reaches the medial dissection over the duodenum, removing the remaining tissue over the area. The hepatic flexure is further dissected down, with the vessel sealer effectively navigating the fibrotic tissue near the kidney. The surgeon then mobilizes the terminal ileum, freeing it from scar tissue from a previous appendectomy, and progresses from an inferior trajectory to connect with the superior dissection. The terminal ileum is prepared to reach the transverse colon for anastomosis, and the lateral white line is dissected using the ligature.

The surgeon reaches the medial dissection over the duodenum, addressing a thin residual tissue layer. The hepatic flexure is further mobilized, with visible proximity to the right kidney. The vessel sealer is used effectively to navigate fibrotic tissue, presumed to be from a previous endoscopic mucosal resection. The terminal ileum is freed from scar tissue due to a prior appendectomy, and mobilized to connect with the transverse colon for anastomosis. The surgeon completes the dissection by addressing the lateral white line using a ligature.

The surgeon completes the final bite to free the colon and proceeds to dissect the small bowel mesentery, which appears thicker than usual. The vessel sealer fails, prompting the use of a bipolar device to assist in the dissection. The surgeon takes the mesentery in leaflets due to its thickness, focusing on the anterior and middle sections, and finishes the transverse colon mesentery up to the predetermined margin.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the colon, freeing it from surrounding tissues, and begins to take the small bowel mesentery, which appears thicker than usual. The vessel sealer fails, prompting the use of bipolar cautery to assist in the dissection. The mesentery is divided in leaflets due to its thickness, focusing on the anterior and middle sections. The transverse colon mesentery is finished up to the marked margin, with a tattoo visible approximately five centimeters proximal to the polyp's extent, ensuring sufficient margin for resection. Perfusion is checked to confirm adequacy.

The surgeon identifies a tattoo marking approximately five centimeters proximal to the polyp's proximal extent, ensuring adequate margin for resection. Perfusion is checked, and the stapling for the anastomosis is performed through the left upper quadrant port, facilitating alignment towards the patient's left side.

The surgeon staples the small bowel from the left upper quadrant port, positioning the anastomosis towards the patient's left. The small bowel is then divided, and the specimen is temporarily placed above the liver.

The surgeon divides the small bowel and places the specimen above the liver. The small bowel is aligned with the colon in an isoparastaltic fashion, followed by the creation of a colotomy and enterotomy.

The surgeon aligns the small bowel to the colon in an isoparastaltic fashion, creating a colotomy and enterotomy. A vascular load stapler is used to join the bowel segments, ensuring no visible bleeding. The anastomosis is sutured closed using a nine-inch V-lock suture, starting at the near crotch and sewing out and around with larger bites.

The surgeon positions the stapler with a vascular load for the anastomosis, ensuring a trajectory that minimizes bleeding. The surgical field shows no signs of bleeding. The surgeon then sutures the anastomosis closed using a nine-inch V-lock suture, starting at the near crotch and sewing out and around, taking larger bites of tissue.

The surgeon completes the transition of the enterotomy closure by stitching around the corner, bringing the stitches to the outside. The enterotomy is canalized towards the far corner, reaching the top to ensure closure. The surgeon then uses a Lembert technique to stitch back down.

The surgeon transitions the stitch to the outside, canalizing the enterotomy towards the far corner and closing it. A Lembert suture technique is used to reinforce the closure, ensuring coverage of the near corner with a second layer. The surgeon then locks the stitch and cuts it. Proline sutures are used to close the inferior robotic trocar sites, and the mesh is exteriorized through a suprapubic incision. The specimen is brought down and exteriorized below the mesh level.

The surgeon performs a Lembert suture technique, covering the near corner with a second layer. The surgical field is well-exposed, allowing clear visualization of the suturing process. A couple of sutures are run back to lock the stitch before cutting.

The surgeon uses a proline suture with a suture passer to close the two inferior robotic trocar sites, ensuring the mesh is not violated a second time. The mesh is exteriorized through a suprapubic incision below the mesh level. The specimen is then brought down and exteriorized below the mesh.
